jokes.text: I said everyone, Sketch-Tron 6000.
jokes.setup: Jack gathering everyone for a meeting
jokes.punchline: Addressing a robot/machine as if it were a person who wasn't listening
jokes.explanation: Perfect 30 Rock absurdism - treating technology as human with corporate efficiency. Economy and character integration are strong.

jokes.text: Tracy, do not mention where you think dinosaurs come from. Do not mention the underwater city of Sauronicon...
jokes.punchline: Tracy's bizarrely specific conspiracy theories about dinosaurs and underwater cities
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - only Tracy would have theories about underwater Sauronicon. Highly original and perfectly integrated.

jokes.text: If you don't volumize my hair, I will choke you to death with your boyfriend's wig!
jokes.setup: Jack asking Jenna to get her crazy yelling out
jokes.punchline: Absurdly violent threat involving hair styling and a boyfriend's wig
jokes.explanation: Peak Jenna narcissism and drama. The specificity of 'boyfriend's wig' elevates it above generic threats.

jokes.text: Frank, how is your armpit thing? Not great. It's almost touching my thigh thing.
jokes.punchline: Grotesque body horror described casually
jokes.explanation: Efficient gross-out humor. Frank's matter-of-fact delivery sells the absurdity.

jokes.text: Remember, everyone, just don't be yourselves.
jokes.setup: Jack's pep talk about making a good impression
jokes.punchline: The complete opposite of typical motivational advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and structure. Captures Jack's corporate cynicism and the dysfunction of the workplace in six words.

jokes.text: Oh, do you need sex advice? Here's a tip. Sometimes a lady likes to leave her blazer on.
jokes.punchline: Liz's awkward assumption and unsexy sex advice
jokes.explanation: Classic Liz awkwardness. The blazer detail is perfectly unglamorous and specific to her character.

jokes.text: That's nice. Actors deserve gifts. Without us, who would present awards to actors?
jokes.setup: Receiving a gift from the network owner
jokes.punchline: Circular logic about actors giving awards to themselves
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of celebrity narcissism. The logic is hilariously self-serving and perfectly Jenna.

jokes.text: The box would make a perfect coffin for my Teddy Bear.
jokes.setup: Tracy excited about getting a computer
jokes.punchline: His priority is making a coffin for a stuffed animal
jokes.explanation: Dark and unexpected. Tracy's childlike attachment combined with death imagery is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: You taking my nephew's virginity.
jokes.setup: Tracy and Jenna listing past conflicts
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of Jenna sleeping with Tracy's underage relative
jokes.explanation: Shocking escalation delivered matter-of-factly. Dark humor that pushes boundaries while staying true to character dynamics.

jokes.text: I want you to selfishly take the best sweatshirt in the world.
jokes.punchline: Describing generosity as selfish while calling a basic sweatshirt the 'best in the world'
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Jenna's inability to be genuinely generous even when trying.

jokes.text: a tiny desk with a miniature pen set on it
jokes.punchline: Including office supplies for an infant
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack - treating a baby like a tiny executive. Character integration is flawless.

jokes.text: I assumed Avery would have a terrifying best friend she'd do stuff like that with.
jokes.setup: Jack asking Liz to go shopping with Avery
jokes.punchline: Liz's assumption about upper-class female friendships
jokes.explanation: Good social observation through Liz's perspective. 'Terrifying best friend' is a perfect descriptor.

jokes.text: Avery does have a sister, but Eugenia is currently institutionalized for nerves... Lesbian...
jokes.setup: Explaining why Avery's sister can't help
jokes.punchline: Old-fashioned euphemisms for mental illness and homosexuality
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about outdated attitudes. The pause before 'Lesbian' sells Jack's discomfort perfectly.

jokes.text: Michael Kors is a friend. We own a gay racehorse together. And I convinced him to make wizard cloaks fashionable for ladies this winter.
jokes.setup: Explaining how Avery hides her pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Absurd celebrity friendship involving gay racehorses and wizard cloak fashion
jokes.explanation: Peak 30 Rock absurdism. 'Gay racehorse' is inspired nonsense, and the wizard cloaks callback later pays off.

jokes.text: It's my fault for asking.
jokes.setup: Jack's elaborate explanation about Michael Kors and wizard cloaks
jokes.punchline: Liz's resigned acceptance that she shouldn't have asked
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man reaction. Economy is excellent - captures the entire dynamic in four words.

jokes.text: Which is the premise of my one-act play The Seahorses of Warwickshire Abbey.
jokes.setup: Liz making a point about workplace gender equality
jokes.punchline: Revealing she's written a pretentious play about male pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant title that captures Liz's pretentious intellectual aspirations. Seahorses are the perfect choice since males actually carry babies.

jokes.text: Oh, I know her. Hey, what ethnicity is she? No one knows.
jokes.punchline: Her ethnicity being unknowable despite her name
jokes.explanation: Sets up a running theme. The absurdity of not knowing someone's ethnicity despite their name being clearly Asian.

jokes.text: Mexican president Felipe Calderon is traveling this week to China to meet with premier Wen Jiabao, and then stopping for a well-deserved vacation in Negril, Jamaica, mon.
jokes.punchline: Ending serious news with 'mon' in a Jamaican accent
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from professional to casual. The accent switch is unexpected and character-specific.

jokes.text: Carol's away a lot.
jokes.punchline: Liz's matter-of-fact admission of loneliness
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character moment. Three words capture Liz's entire romantic situation and loneliness.

jokes.text: And that's not racist because I don't know what she is.
jokes.punchline: Justifying potential racism with ignorance
jokes.explanation: Dark satire of corporate speak and casual racism. Jack's logic is hilariously flawed.

jokes.text: You can tell a lot about someone by their handshake. You are confident. And you ate dinner in front of a mirror last night.
jokes.setup: Jack's philosophy about handshakes revealing character
jokes.punchline: Specific, pathetic detail about Liz eating alone
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection from generic to brutally specific. The mirror detail is perfectly sad and Liz-like.

jokes.text: I'm a hugger. No. Doesn't count as a hug unless it goes on for ten seconds.
jokes.setup: Hank introducing himself as a hugger
jokes.punchline: Insisting on uncomfortably long hugs with specific timing
jokes.explanation: Establishes Hank's character perfectly. The ten-second rule makes normal affection creepy.

jokes.text: Or 'hey, idiot,' like my wife does. I'm kidding. She's an angel.
jokes.setup: Hank saying to call him by name
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecating joke followed by sweet correction
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Hank's folksy charm. Misdirection works but isn't groundbreaking.

jokes.text: This is no longer the executive dining room. It's the Everyone dining room.
jokes.punchline: Renaming the dining room with childlike simplicity
jokes.explanation: Good character work. 'Everyone dining room' captures Hank's folksy, simple approach to corporate culture.

jokes.text: This is where we used to hold retirement parties. The balcony below is probably still littered with stripper bones.
jokes.setup: Jack commenting on the dining room's history
jokes.punchline: Implication that strippers died at corporate parties
jokes.explanation: Darkly hilarious escalation. 'Stripper bones' is an inspired phrase that suggests horrific corporate excess.

jokes.text: Do you know who came up with the idea for the DVR? It was the guy who drives me home every night. The original name for the DVR was 'The Thing Carlos Thought Of.'
jokes.punchline: His driver Carlos invented it with the most literal name possible
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor. 'The Thing Carlos Thought Of' is hilariously literal and perfectly fits Hank's folksy management style.

jokes.text: What is this green stuff? Wipe it off. Wipe it off!
jokes.punchline: Panicked reaction to what's presumably pesto or herbs
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy showing Hank's simple tastes. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: I like pradort bag.
jokes.punchline: Carmen's mispronunciation of 'Prada' as 'pradort'
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that adds to Carmen's mysterious background. Economy is good but humor is basic.

jokes.text: I love their creepy little hands and the way they crawl fast, like a rat monster.
jokes.punchline: Describing babies in horrifying terms
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of expected baby talk. 'Rat monster' is inspired and reveals Carmen's sinister nature.

jokes.text: Some dude jacked me, and now his sperm is growing in my stomach.
jokes.punchline: Clinical, unsexy description of conception
jokes.explanation: Perfect Liz awkwardness. The clinical language combined with 'jacked me' is hilariously uncomfortable.

jokes.text: That's now how babies are made.
jokes.punchline: Carmen's matter-of-fact correction
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man response. Economy is perfect - five words deflate Liz's entire explanation.

jokes.text: So by the law of verbal traps, you have to do it.
jokes.setup: Carmen getting Liz to agree to be in her story
jokes.punchline: Invoking a made-up legal principle
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor. 'Law of verbal traps' sounds official but is complete nonsense, perfect for Carmen's manipulative nature.

jokes.text: even though all their advertising suggests it is a place for groups of friends to have a good time.
jokes.setup: Liz saying she'll go to the popcorn place alone
jokes.punchline: Noting how the advertising emphasizes group fun, highlighting her loneliness
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor that doubles as character insight. Captures the sadness of going alone to 'fun group' places.

jokes.text: Good sweatshirt to you. How are you sweatshirting this sweatshirt?
jokes.punchline: Replacing normal words with 'sweatshirt' in greetings
jokes.explanation: Pure Tracy absurdism. His obsession with the sweatshirt has infected his entire vocabulary.

jokes.text: Was I supposed to throw up in something of mine?
jokes.setup: Tracy accusing Jenna of stealing his grandmother's jewelry chest
jokes.punchline: Jenna's selfish justification for using it as a vomit receptacle
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jenna logic - completely self-centered and missing the sentimental value. Character integration is flawless.

jokes.text: You'll never get her! Stop! Sweatshirty is a boy!
jokes.setup: Tracy and Jenna fighting over the sweatshirt
jokes.punchline: Tracy naming the sweatshirt and assigning it gender
jokes.explanation: Escalation of Tracy's sweatshirt obsession. 'Sweatshirty' and the gender assignment are perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Is she doing an expose on how feminism has led to a happiness crisis among educated women?
jokes.punchline: Suggesting a specific anti-feminist story angle
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kenneth - conservative views delivered innocently. The specificity suggests he's thought about this topic.

jokes.text: Lemon told me this morning she got jacked big time.
jokes.punchline: Misusing 'jacked' to mean impregnated
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Liz's earlier language. Tracy's interpretation makes it even more crude.

jokes.text: Like a bloodhound. Perhaps literally. We still don't know her genetic background.
jokes.punchline: Suggesting she might literally be part dog due to unknown ethnicity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Carmen mystery. The literal bloodhound possibility is absurd but fits the show's logic.

jokes.text: because I have had my period for the last 61 days.
jokes.setup: Liz explaining why she can't be pregnant
jokes.punchline: Oversharing about extremely long menstruation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Liz oversharing. The specific number '61 days' makes it horrifyingly real and specific.

jokes.text: Don Geiss gave me this watch for firing a man on his deathbed!
jokes.punchline: Being rewarded for ultimate corporate cruelty
jokes.explanation: Peak dark corporate humor. The image of firing someone on their deathbed being rewarded is perfectly horrifying and Jack-like.

jokes.text: How come when I try to get you to go to a murder-mystery party with me, it's all, 'we're business colleagues, Lemon'?
jokes.punchline: Liz pointing out his selective definition of friendship
jokes.explanation: Good relationship comedy. The murder-mystery party detail is perfectly Liz - nerdy and social but awkward.

jokes.text: I don't know why I ever chose you as a friend. Let's just be clear about this... I chose you.
jokes.setup: Jack lamenting choosing Liz as a friend
jokes.punchline: Liz asserting she chose him, leading to Jack's immediate correction
jokes.explanation: Good back-and-forth showing their competitive dynamic. Jack's immediate need to assert dominance is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: May I kiss your stomach? Absolutely not. Hello, beautiful. It's Uncle Wutzy.
jokes.setup: Kenneth wanting to interact with the fake baby
jokes.punchline: Liz rejecting the kiss but Kenneth talking to her stomach anyway, using a ridiculous nickname
jokes.explanation: Perfectly awkward Kenneth moment. 'Uncle Wutzy' is an inspired ridiculous nickname that only Kenneth would use.

jokes.text: And my sidekick is a hot black chick who can read minds.
jokes.punchline: Reducing the female character to crude stereotypes and magical powers
jokes.explanation: Decent satire of bad TV writing. The combination of objectification and random superpowers is effectively absurd.

jokes.text: We need a button that switches from pornography to basketball immediately. What you have isn't fast enough. My kids are starting to ask questions that I can't answer.
jokes.punchline: Admitting to watching pornography around children
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about inappropriate parenting. The specific basketball/pornography switch is uncomfortably real.

jokes.text: Like nudity, or soccer.
jokes.setup: Describing what the black bar would block
jokes.punchline: Equating soccer with nudity as equally offensive
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation. Treating soccer as morally equivalent to nudity is perfectly American and absurd.

jokes.text: Congratulations. Worst so far!
jokes.punchline: Jack's brutally honest assessment delivered cheerfully
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jack - maintaining politeness while being devastating. The cheerful delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I will need to eat your umbilical cord.
jokes.punchline: Casually mentioning cannibalistic birthing ritual
jokes.explanation: Darkly hilarious Frank moment. The casual delivery of something so grotesque is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: It'll turn the child into a Dracula.
jokes.setup: Tracy explaining why they shouldn't stress pregnant Liz
jokes.punchline: Claiming stress causes vampirism in babies
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tracy logic - treating vampirism as a medical condition. 'A Dracula' instead of 'a vampire' is inspired.

jokes.text: Remember, you're eating for two. Well, I guess two egg sandwiches... Times two is four.
jokes.setup: Someone telling pregnant Liz she's eating for two
jokes.punchline: Liz's mathematical interpretation leading to four sandwiches
jokes.explanation: Good Liz moment showing her food obsession and literal thinking. The math escalation works well.

jokes.text: I had naan.
jokes.setup: Hank mentioning new experiences and Indian food
jokes.punchline: Treating naan bread as exotic and noteworthy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - finding the most basic Indian food exotic. Economy is excellent for character development.

jokes.text: I am a six sigma black belt ultra, with the groin branding to prove it.
jokes.punchline: Claiming extreme corporate certification with physical proof
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jack corporate parody. 'Groin branding' escalates corporate culture to absurd extremes perfectly.

jokes.text: What's six sigma? Rock band?
jokes.punchline: Hank's complete ignorance of business terminology
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Jack's corporate posturing. Hank's folksy ignorance undermines everything Jack values.

jokes.text: Television on. Channel, NBC. [nothing happens] That shouldn't happen. TV on! Voice activation, or 'vo-act...' [still nothing] Un-mute! Low volume. Low volume! TV mute!
jokes.punchline: The technology completely failing to work while he gets increasingly desperate
jokes.explanation: Classic tech failure comedy. Jack's increasing desperation and made-up terms like 'vo-act' sell the awkwardness.

jokes.text: You could call it 'Blabar.'
jokes.setup: Hank liking the black bar idea
jokes.punchline: Terrible portmanteau name combining 'black' and 'bar'
jokes.explanation: Good corporate naming satire. 'Blabar' is realistically terrible branding that sounds like a real product.

jokes.text: Not what my uncle does when he gets a hitchhiker over a barrel, I'll tell you that.
jokes.setup: Kenneth saying what he won't do when having leverage
jokes.punchline: Implying his uncle murders hitchhikers
jokes.explanation: Dark Kenneth humor at its best. The casual reference to family murder delivered innocently is perfect character work.

jokes.text: You didn't steal anything from me, sir. Last time I checked, best friends can't steal from each other.
jokes.setup: Jack trying to explain business leverage
jokes.punchline: Kenneth's innocent view of friendship defeating corporate cynicism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kenneth innocence. His genuine friendship view frustrates Jack's cynical business worldview perfectly.

jokes.text: This isn't how it works! You're the one being a silly Simon!
jokes.punchline: Jack reduced to using Kenneth's childish language
jokes.explanation: Excellent role reversal. Jack being reduced to Kenneth's innocent language shows complete frustration.

jokes.text: April 20th, Hitler's birthday.
jokes.setup: Carmen asking when Liz is due
jokes.punchline: Associating the fake baby's due date with Hitler
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with perfect economy. Only Liz would know and blurt out Hitler's birthday in this context.

jokes.text: Dr. Rufus T. Barleysheath.
jokes.punchline: Completely made-up, ridiculous-sounding doctor name
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist name that sounds both medical and completely fake. 'Barleysheath' is particularly inspired.

jokes.text: In my vagina.
jokes.setup: Carmen asking where the baby was conceived
jokes.punchline: Liz's literal, clinical, and inappropriate answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Liz awkwardness. Taking the question literally and giving the most clinical possible answer is peak character.

jokes.text: Rufus T. Barleysheath.
jokes.punchline: Liz reusing the fake doctor name
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Liz's panic and lack of preparation. Reusing names shows she's not good at lying.

jokes.text: No...way that I'm not.
jokes.setup: Carmen asking directly if she's pregnant
jokes.punchline: Liz's awkward pause and double negative
jokes.explanation: Good awkward comedy. The pause and grammatical confusion perfectly show Liz's discomfort with lying.

jokes.text: She's a blonde. Don't you know there's a war going on?
jokes.setup: Carmen asking why Liz is helping Avery
jokes.punchline: Treating hair color as a military conflict
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation. Treating blonde vs. brunette as warfare is perfectly silly and fits Carmen's intensity.

jokes.text: First of all, Jack means nothing to me. Shoot him in the throat and let the dogs eat him. I don't care.
jokes.punchline: Extremely violent imagery delivered casually
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation that's unexpected from Liz. The specific 'dogs eat him' imagery is hilariously violent.

jokes.text: where you bare your stomach like the pregnant bitch that you are.
jokes.punchline: Suddenly dropping the friendly facade with vicious language
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Carmen's true nature. The sudden shift from professional to vicious is effective.

jokes.text: I can get you into a restaurant where you watch a child play with a bunny, then you eat the bunny.
jokes.punchline: Describing an extremely disturbing dining experience
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark absurdist humor. The psychological horror of the scenario is perfectly crafted and quotable.

jokes.text: Isn't that just Easter?
jokes.punchline: Kenneth connecting it innocently to Easter traditions
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Jack's elaborate corruption with innocent logic. Economy is flawless - three words destroy the entire premise.

jokes.text: Blackmail me. Demand to sleep with Avery. Hit me. Hit me in the face!
jokes.setup: Jack desperately trying to get Kenneth to leverage the situation
jokes.punchline: Escalates to increasingly desperate and inappropriate demands
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation showing Jack's desperation. The progression from professional to personal to physical is well-crafted.

jokes.text: Does he also solve mazes by starting from the end?
jokes.setup: Jack frustrated with Hank's unconventional thinking
jokes.punchline: Comparing Hank's management style to backwards maze solving
jokes.explanation: Good metaphor for unconventional thinking. The maze analogy perfectly captures Jack's frustration with Hank's methods.

jokes.text: Oh, damn my krav maga training and lightning reflexes!
jokes.setup: Kenneth trying to hit Jack but Jack automatically blocking
jokes.punchline: Jack's elaborate excuse for his defensive reaction
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy with character detail. Jack having krav maga training is perfectly executive and ridiculous.

jokes.text: Release me, you hillbilly Circe!
jokes.setup: Kenneth having Jack in some kind of hold
jokes.punchline: Combining hillbilly stereotypes with Greek mythology
jokes.explanation: Brilliant combining of high and low culture. Only Jack would reference Greek mythology while being defeated by Kenneth.

jokes.text: Rufus T. Barleysheath is kicking.
jokes.punchline: Using the fake doctor name as the baby's name
jokes.explanation: Decent callback showing Liz committing to her terrible lies. The escalation to the fake baby kicking works.

jokes.text: I don't fly, but I got my own bus with a pool table in it.
jokes.setup: Hank explaining his travel to Philadelphia
jokes.punchline: Having a custom bus with recreational equipment
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing Hank's folksy wealth. The pool table bus is perfectly absurd and character-specific.

jokes.text: We'll drive to Branson together sometime, take in a Yakov Smirnoff show. Doesn't Russia sound like a crazy place?
jokes.punchline: Treating outdated comedy and Soviet jokes as current entertainment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character work. Branson and Yakov Smirnoff perfectly capture his outdated tastes.

jokes.text: Oh, I didn't notice you there, son. You do not have a lot of charisma.
jokes.punchline: Immediately insulting Kenneth's presence
jokes.explanation: Good awkward moment. Hank's blunt honesty about Kenneth's lack of charisma is perfectly uncomfortable.

jokes.text: You have a reputation, Jack, as a shark. Kabletown, we're not sharks. We're more like... Whatever the friendliest fish is.
jokes.punchline: Not knowing what friendly fish exist
jokes.explanation: Good Hank moment showing his folksy but limited knowledge. The corporate metaphor falling apart is perfect.

jokes.text: I'm not a science guy.
jokes.punchline: Understated admission of ignorance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank understatement. Treating fish knowledge as advanced science is perfectly character-appropriate.

jokes.text: It doesn't count as a hug unless it goes on for ten seconds. You got that right, son. One...Mississippi...
jokes.setup: Kenneth and Hank starting their hug
jokes.punchline: Both adhering to Hank's uncomfortable hug duration rule
jokes.explanation: Good callback creating maximum awkwardness. The countdown makes the uncomfortable hug even worse.

jokes.text: I will miss my dear friends here on basic cable.
jokes.punchline: Condescending reference to basic cable while leaving
jokes.explanation: Good Carmen exit showing her condescending nature. The 'basic cable' dig is perfectly passive-aggressive.

jokes.text: Oh, Jack, that's so gayballs.
jokes.setup: Jack naming his baby after Liz
jokes.punchline: Liz's inappropriate response using a made-up crude term
jokes.explanation: Good Liz awkwardness. Using 'gayballs' for a sweet moment is perfectly inappropriate and character-specific.

jokes.text: Do you need some liniment rubbed on your perineum?
jokes.setup: Writers being nice to pregnant Liz
jokes.punchline: Frank's medically specific and inappropriate offer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank gross-out humor. The medical terminology makes his inappropriate offer even more uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Help him for real. It takes the same amount of time.
jokes.setup: Liz explaining she was just pretending to help Jack
jokes.punchline: Tracy's logic that real help and fake help require equal effort
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Tracy logic. The observation is both absurd and accidentally profound about human relationships.

jokes.text: And she ordered us around like we were a couple of normals.
jokes.punchline: Treating being 'normal' as an insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jenna narcissism. Using 'normals' as a derogatory term perfectly captures her self-importance.

jokes.text: Good God! I have been punished. These are yours to keep.
jokes.setup: Tracy and Jenna apparently attacking Liz with nunchucks
jokes.punchline: Liz's dramatic surrender and gift of the weapons
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy aftermath. Liz immediately surrendering and giving them the weapons is perfectly defeatist.

jokes.text: What if we executed someone live on TV?
jokes.punchline: Casually suggesting televised murder as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation of terrible TV ideas. The casualness of the suggestion is effective but not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: And then I pull off my mask and I'm a lizard person too. Blackout. End of episode.
jokes.punchline: Ending with conspiracy theory revelation and theatrical terminology
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about bad TV writing. The 'blackout' reference is a nice theatrical touch.
